title: 埃及十字架之谜
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\27-埃及十字架之谜-埃勒里奎因.txt
summary:
Ellery Quin analyzes a checkers game found in Breda's study to reconstruct the circumstances surrounding Breda's murder. Although Stolings had previously observed Breda playing alone, Ellery argues that the state of the board proves Breda was playing against a real opponent. The game was highly unbalanced, with the Black pieces holding a dominant advantage of two kings and two pieces against Red's two weak pawns. Ellery concludes that a master player like Breda would never have engaged in an intentionally lopsided practice game, implying the presence of a visitor.

By examining the board, Ellery determines that Breda was playing as Black and sitting in the chair closest to the desk. He notes that a red pawn had reached the black king's line but had not yet been crowned. This missing red pawn was discovered near Breda's body at the totem pole, and a red mark was found on Breda's palm, suggesting he was holding the piece to crown the opponent's pawn when he was interrupted.

Ellery connects these findings to the crime scene, noting a bloodstain on the carpet behind Breda's chair. He posits that Breda was attacked from the front while attempting to crown the pawn, causing him to fall backward. This leads Ellery to conclude that the murderer was Breda's opponent. He refutes Professor Yadley's suggestion that an accomplice or Crosetti attacked Breda from behind while an innocent visitor played chess; Ellery argues that Crosetti would not have attacked in front of a witness, nor would he have left a witness unharmed. Therefore, the murderer must have been the person playing the game with Breda.
